name: Phase 2 — Departure (Three Approaches, Pressure Clock, Ending)

text.content:
Read aloud at the end of the investigation, when the party has identified Cindren & Vhal, has chased the Gilst and Moraven threads as far as Phase 2 supports, and Caelith is ready to move them to the choice of approach. Setting: the Refectory, the morning of the third day. The evidence is still on the long table. Outside, the city is going about its day.

Caelith sets the copied countermark beside the clipped coin.

Tower. Waves.

"Cindren & Vhal," he says. "Bonded factors. Respectable enough for clean rooms. Useful enough for dirty ones."

He pins the eastern coast on a small map between the linen squares.

"Their active office is in Rel Astra. The Old Harbor Ward. The senior partners answer to no one in this kingdom or the next, in any way the law would let us reach them."

Outside, bells ring.

"I do not know whether Haskur Vandrell is there."

"But someone there was ready for him."

A pause. He looks at each of you in turn.

"And if we have learned this much by morning… then by nightfall they will know we learned it."

Transitions to: the Three Travel Approaches scene (next page). Caelith now waits for the party to choose.

GM note. This is the speech that crosses the line. Until now, the party has been investigating at home; with this read-aloud, the question pivots from "what happened?" to "how soon do we leave?" The Pressure Clock should already be ticking when this is read; if it has not advanced past segment 2, advance it by one segment as the speech ends — the conspirators are not waiting for the party to be ready.

Once the party has identified Cindren & Vhal, has chased the Gilst and Moraven threads to the level Phase 2 supports, and has either taken or refused Arthen Moll's offer, Caelith presents three ways east. The party may also propose a fourth — Caelith will hear it.

The setting

Caelith asks the party to meet him in the Old Council Chamber (U4) — the disused upper-floor room with the ring of nine carved chairs. He has Elsbet open it for the occasion. The chamber is cold; the hearth has been lit and is just beginning to warm the room. Trina is present, seated in the second-oldest chair (by founding-district name).

On the round oak table, Caelith has set out three small leather folios. Each contains the paperwork for one approach. He pushes them toward the party in turn.

Approach 1 — Official Delegation

The party travels east as accredited representatives of the Yeomanry, with Council-issued credentials, a written charge to "inquire into Aerdi-Yeomanry trade-clearing irregularities," and the standing of foreign diplomatic visitors. Caelith would not lead the delegation himself; the formal head would be a junior councilman willing to lend his name (Caelith has one in mind). The party would travel with the councilman, two clerks, and a small Yeomanry guard escort.

What it costs

  • Speed. Slow. The delegation's speed is the speed of paperwork. Eight to ten days to Hardby, another eight to ten to Rel Astra, with formal stops at every customs post and a written report back to Loftwick at each.
  • Visibility. The conspiracy will know the party is coming and will know exactly when. Cindren & Vhal can prepare; witnesses can vanish; documents can be sanitized.
  • Risk to Caelith. Filing the formal request requires Caelith to use four favors with the public-closure faction (Moll's allies). He can spend the favors. He would prefer not to.

What it buys

  • Legal standing. Aerdi authorities cannot detain the party without diplomatic incident. Local watch in Rel Astra will treat the party with formal courtesy.
  • Access. Cindren & Vhal cannot refuse to receive the party at their main office; the firm is bound by Aerdi commercial law to receive accredited foreign inquirers.
  • Witness protection. If the party finds Tamsin Moraven alive, she can travel back to Loftwick under diplomatic cover.

Pressure Clock interaction

This approach advances the Pressure Clock by 2 segments immediately (filing the credentials triggers political reactions in Loftwick). It then advances by 1 segment per week of travel as Cindren & Vhal use the time.

Approach 2 — Commercial Cover

The party travels east as a private trade escort — a small mercantile delegation pretending to be assessing eastern grain markets for a Yeomanry investor. Caelith arranges the cover through a Loftwick wool merchant who owes him a favor. The party carries real samples of Yeomanry wool and fleece, a real (small) line of letters of introduction to Hardby and Rel Astran wool buyers, and Trina's letter of introduction to Castrian Vell the poet.

What it costs

  • Discipline. The party must play the role consistently. Anyone who breaks cover risks the entire group. PCs who cannot keep a story straight will struggle.
  • Plausibility budget. If a PC is unmistakably a paladin in plate, or a gnome bard known to the Hardby tavern circuit by reputation, the cover thins. The GM may require Deception checks at toll-gates and at firm receptions.
  • No diplomatic cover. If the cover breaks, the party is just travelers — Aerdi authorities can hold them on suspicion of false identity for up to thirty days.

What it buys

  • Speed. Six to seven days to Hardby, four to five to Rel Astra. Faster than Approach 1.
  • Plausible access. Wool merchants visit counting houses for credit lines. Cindren & Vhal will receive the party as a routine business call. Real conversation is possible.
  • Trina's network. Castrian Vell is a poet with a sponsor in Hardby and an ear at four salons. He will host the party, ask the right questions of the right people, and tell them what he hears. He cannot prove anything; he can give them maps.
  • Veska Maelan in Hardby. Caelith's letter of introduction to Veska Maelan (Phase 1 forward hook) functions as a backup channel; Maelan can read the Cindren & Vhal Hardby chops better than anyone outside the firm.

Pressure Clock interaction

This approach does not advance the Pressure Clock at departure. It advances by 1 segment if the cover is seriously broken at any toll-gate or firm reception (GM call), and by 1 segment if the party visits Castrian Vell openly more than twice in Hardby.

Approach 3 — Quiet Pursuit

The party travels east as a small fast-moving group with no official capacity — fresh horses, light packs, no documents beyond personal papers. Caelith provides a small purse of Yeomanry trade slugs and three letters of safe-passage drawn for false names of the party's choosing.

What it costs

  • No cover. If they are caught east of Hardby with the false papers, the local watch will not believe them, and there will be no diplomatic apparatus to extract them. Caelith says, plainly, "If you are taken, I cannot reach you."
  • Limited resources. No baggage train, no clerk, no formal staff. Whatever they need they carry.
  • Limited access. Cindren & Vhal will not receive a small group of travelers without an introduction. The party must find a side door.

What it buys

  • Speed. Four to five days to Hardby, three to Rel Astra. The fastest approach by a wide margin.
  • Invisibility. The conspiracy does not know the party is moving until the party arrives in Hardby. This is the only approach that surprises them.
  • Optionality. The party can change tactics on arrival. They can buy a cover in Hardby (Trina's letter to Castrian Vell still functions), or they can present themselves as private inquirers to anyone they choose.

Pressure Clock interaction

This approach does not advance the Pressure Clock at departure. It advances by 2 segments if the party is recognized in Hardby (e.g., by a former contact, a Watch officer, or a Cindren & Vhal courier) and they cannot quickly leave the city.

Caelith's framing of the trade-off

When the three folios are on the table and the party asks Caelith to help them choose, he sets one finger on each in turn and says, very evenly:

"I can give you authority, cover, or distance. I cannot give you all three."

That is the entire shape of the choice. The rest is the party's.

Caelith's recommendation

If pressed for a recommendation rather than the framing, Caelith adds: "Each path costs me something I would prefer not to spend, and each path costs you something I would prefer not to ask of you. I would say only this: if your party can keep its mouth shut for two weeks, the second path is the path I would take. If your party cannot, the first path will keep you alive longer than the third, even if it makes the work slower."

Trina, if asked, says nothing about the first or third paths. She says, of the second: "Castrian's house is mine in everything but address. He will know you are mine. Do not embarrass him."

What if the party proposes a fourth approach

Caelith will listen to any proposal. Examples that may come up:

  • Magical travel. If the party has access to teleportation circle or similar, Caelith does not have one tuned to Hardby or Rel Astra (and he is wary of the Aerdi authorities reading magical arrivals on the harbor commission's wards). He will not provide one. The party may have their own resource.
  • Sea passage from Saltmarsh. Adds two weeks but bypasses Hardby entirely. Caelith allows it; Cindren & Vhal have no sea-leg in Saltmarsh.
  • Splitting the party. Caelith advises against. "You are stronger than my agents because you are together. I would not separate you."

The Pressure Clock measures how quickly Phase 2 closes around the party. The conspiracy does not have a master plan that triggers at a specific date; it has a tolerance for visibility, and the more visible the party is, the faster that tolerance runs out. The clock is the GM's tool to keep Phase 2 from sprawling into endless investigation, and to make the party choose a travel approach before circumstances force the choice for them.

Mechanic

The clock has six segments. It begins at 0 the morning of the evidence review. The GM advances the clock by adding segments as below. When the clock reaches each milestone, the GM resolves the corresponding event before continuing the session.

Triggers — what advances the clock

TriggerSegments
The party visits the Cindren & Vhal Loftwick satellite (Wick Lane) and shows Hesren Vesh the Vandrell endorsement+1
The party confronts Reln Pollow at his contract-physician office+1
The party files a public watch escalation on Tamsin Moraven's case (rather than letting Caelith reopen it quietly)+1
The party declines or defers Arthen Moll's offer+1 (in addition to triggering Sarth)
Each full session day in which the party makes no investigative progress+1
Merev Sarth retrieval attempt resolves+1 regardless of outcome
The party files credentials for the Official Delegation approach+2 (immediate)
Per week of travel under the Official Delegation approach+1
Cover seriously broken under Commercial Cover (GM call)+1
Party recognized in Hardby under Quiet Pursuit and cannot leave quickly+2

The clock at a glance — six escalating rhythms

The clock fires in roughly this order. Each segment is a kind of pressure; the detailed consequences below name what specifically happens when the GM advances to that segment.

  1. Inventory tampering — someone has been in the strong room.
  2. Anonymous warning — a hand reaches for Trina before it reaches the party.
  3. Public rumor — the matter becomes street-talk.
  4. Witness loss — a person, a name, or a paperwork trail vanishes.
  5. Council hearing — the closure motion is filed.
  6. The closing event — the door shuts on Phase 2.

Note that two of the most dramatic Phase 2 events — Arthen Moll's pressure visit and Merev Sarth's retrieval attempt — are scheduled scenes triggered by the party's choices, not clock segments. They overlap with the clock's progress; they do not depend on it.

Milestones — what happens at each segment

Segment 1 — Inventory tampering

Caelith comes down to the Strong Room one morning and finds one item moved exactly one inch from where he set it the night before. Nothing is missing. Nothing is added. The tampering is a signal, not a theft. Caelith says nothing for a long moment, then closes the chest and walks back upstairs.

GM: the tamperer is not Merev Sarth — Sarth's retrieval is a scheduled scene. This is someone Caelith trusts; an Audit Hall clerk, a militia guard with strong-room access, or a member of his own staff. The tamperer was sent only to confirm the evidence is still here. The fact that it is, in the morning, gives the conspirators their next data point.

Segment 2 — Anonymous warning

An anonymous note is slipped to the door of Trina's townhouse, addressed to her in a careful hand: "Mistress Alvere — your friends in residence at the Little Palace are pursuing a matter that does not warrant your kindness. Withdraw your hospitality. — A neighbor." Trina shows it to the party in private and says, mildly: "The neighbor is mistaken." She does not withdraw.

Segment 3 — Public rumor

By the next morning, the matter is on the streets. A small anonymous broadsheet appears at four printers' shops asking, in the prose-poetry style of Loftwick civic gossip, "What does the Audit Hall keep in its little palace these days, and who is foreign in this question?" The Folio of the Mere quietly declines to reprint it. Three other smaller sheets reprint it twice. The party becomes recognizable in the city in a way they were not the day before.

Segment 4 — Witness loss

One of the party's threads goes cold. GM picks one — pick the one that hurts most given what they've been chasing:

  • Sergeant Halir Anwic is removed from Tamsin Moraven's case "pending review of his caseload." A new sergeant — pleasant, slow, uninterested — is assigned. Anwic finds the party privately and hands over his last notes as a gift before he goes.
  • Reln Pollow is found dead in his Wick Lane office, certified by another contract physician (not the watch). The party loses Pollow as a witness. Caelith, on hearing, says only: "I should have moved him sooner. I will remember."
  • Gilst's room is cleaned. Whoever the party was racing has reached Doman Reith first. The hollowed Constitutional Commentaries are gone. The throat-lozenge tin is gone. The locket is gone. Reith looks ten years older. He says, "I am sorry. They had a writ."

Segment 5 — Council hearing

Arthen Moll's faction successfully files a Council motion of Civic Reconciliation Closure on the Vandrell matter. The motion is procedural: it does not stop private inquiry, but it formally declares the matter administratively closed, which means any Yeomanry institution that continues to assist the party is doing so against the published Council position. Caelith loses three favors. Trina loses two. The Little Palace remains hospitable, but the Audit Hall withdraws clerical support. The party must leave Loftwick within 48 hours or accept that Caelith cannot continue to assist them inside the city.

At the same time, Hesren Vesh — the Cindren & Vhal Loftwick agent — departs Loftwick by night for Hardby "at the firm's recall." The Loftwick satellite office closes its public window the following morning.

Segment 6 — The closing event

One of the following, GM choice — pick the one that hits hardest given what the party has invested in:

  • Tamsin Moraven is confirmed dead. A body is recovered from the Sheldomar near Hardby. The cause of death is officially listed as drowning. The Yeomanry watch will not reopen the case. Trina, on hearing, sits down for a long time. (Use only if the GM is willing to commit Moraven to dead; otherwise prefer one of the other two below.)
  • Caelith is recalled. The Duke summons Caelith for a private audience that becomes an extended "consultation." In practical terms, Caelith is removed from the work for two weeks. The party loses their primary contact during that window.
  • Vellin Moraven is taken. Tamsin's husband disappears from his small parlor on Tannerway. Ailen is now alone in Hardby. The Moravens become a hostage thread in Phase 3.

Whichever the GM chooses, segment 6 makes Phase 2's investigation cease to be the most important thing the party has to do. Phase 3 begins under worse conditions than it would have begun an hour earlier.

How to use the clock at the table

  • Show the players the clock face. Do not hide it. The pressure should be felt, not ambushed.
  • When you advance, say which trigger fired. Do not punish surprise.
  • If a player asks "is this going to advance the clock?" — yes, you should tell them, and they should be allowed to back out of the action if they wish. Investigation games where the cost is hidden grow boring fast.
  • The clock is not a punishment device. It is a pacing device. If the party is moving with reasonable speed and reasonable visibility, they will reach Phase 3 with the clock at 3-4 segments. That is the intended outcome.

Read aloud at the moment the party leaves Loftwick under whichever travel approach they chose. The text below is approach-agnostic — it works whether they ride out at noon as a delegation or before dawn as nameless travelers. Adjust the small details (number of horses, presence of clerks, the form of farewell) to match.

Loftwick is at its quietest in the hour you leave. The Civic Way is broad and pale; the lamps along the Audit Hall are still burning even though the sky has gone the colour of new milk. The footmen at the Little Palace gate raise their lanterns once and lower them, the old Yeomanry sign for safe travel.

Caelith stands at the gate. He does not embrace anyone. He steps forward to the lead rider and offers a small flat brass token — the same one he showed in the Strong Room, a quill above a closed gate. He says, "If you find the man who underwrote him, write to me at this house, with this on the seal. Three days from now I will be in this office or its successor; the token will reach me either way."

Trina is at his shoulder. She does not speak; she has already said what she needed to say, last night, in private, to whichever of you was the last person she walked with. The silver leaf at her collar catches the early light. She lifts a hand, briefly, and lowers it.

The road east bends out of the Civic Way and onto the Wool Road. By the time the city walls drop behind you, the lamps of the Audit Hall have gone out. You travel for an hour before any of you speaks, and when you do, you find that no one has yet asked the question that you all carry: what was waiting for him in Rel Astra, and is it still waiting now.

Hardby is six days away if you press the horses. Rel Astra is, on the maps, eleven days. On Caelith's quiet calculation, it is closer than that. He has not said by how much.

You do not know — none of you know — whether Haskur Vandrell is in Rel Astra, or in a quiet inland house, or already two countries further on. You know only that someone in Rel Astra was prepared for him, and that the same hand that put the chop on his escrow has been writing on Yeomanry paperwork for at least four years.

You ride east. The road is good for as long as the Yeomanry maintains it.

Where Phase 3 picks up

Phase 3 begins with the party's arrival at the gates of Hardby — whichever approach they took. The Hardby of Phase 3 is the chain's middle link: the Cindren & Vhal branch is here, Veska Maelan the trade-clearing notary is here, Castrian Vell is here, Tamsin Moraven may be here, and the broker who hired Merev Sarth is here. Phase 3 does not resolve the conspiracy. It does, however, name the broker, recover or confirm Moraven's fate, and produce the document that puts the senior partners of Cindren & Vhal Rel Astra inside the conspiracy by their own hand.

Phase 4 is Rel Astra.
